attributes.stem:
A three-month-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of persistent oral thrush and diarrhoea. He has had two previous hospital admissions for pneumonia. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. He has no family history of note. His weight has fallen from the 50th to the 2nd centile since birth. His immunisations are up to date including BCG at birth.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 150/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 45/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. He has extensive oral candidiasis and scattered crackles throughout both lung fields. His chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates and absence of thymic shadow.

Laboratory investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 95 g/L (105-135)
  • White cell count 2.1 x 10^9/L (6.0-17.5)
  • Lymphocyte count 0.2 x 10^9/L (3.0-9.5)
  • Neutrophil count 1.8 x 10^9/L (1.5-8.5)
  • IgG 0.5 g/L (2.5-7.5)
  • IgA <0.1 g/L (0.1-0.5)
  • IgM <0.1 g/L (0.2-1.0)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This infant presents with classic features of severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID): early-onset recurrent severe infections, failure to thrive, and profound lymphopenia. The absence of thymic shadow on chest X-ray is characteristic of severe combined immunodeficiency. The combination of very low lymphocyte count and hypogammaglobulinaemia indicates defects in both cellular and humoral immunity. The presence of oral candidiasis suggests defective T-cell immunity. The BCG vaccination in this patient is concerning as live vaccines can cause disseminated infection in SCID.

Transient hypogammaglobulinaemia of infancy typically presents after six months of age when maternal antibodies have waned. These patients usually have normal lymphocyte counts and less severe presentations.

Common variable immunodeficiency typically presents in older children or young adults with recurrent sinopulmonary infections. The lymphocyte count is usually normal, with the primary defect being in antibody production.

X-linked HyperIgM syndrome is characterised by normal or elevated IgM levels with low IgG and IgA. While patients can present with opportunistic infections, they typically have normal lymphocyte counts.

Chronic granulomatous disease is characterised by defective neutrophil function rather than lymphopenia. Patients typically present with recurrent bacterial and fungal infections, particularly with catalase-positive organisms such as Staphylococcus aureus, Burkholderia cepacia, Aspergillus species, and Nocardia species, but would have normal lymphocyte counts.

SCID is a paediatric emergency requiring prompt recognition and management. Treatment includes isolation in a protected environment, prophylactic antimicrobials, immunoglobulin replacement, and definitive therapy with haematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
learningPoints: Severe combined immunodeficiency typically presents in infancy with recurrent severe infections and failure to thrive.
Absence of thymic shadow on chest X-ray is a characteristic finding in SCID.
Live vaccines are contraindicated in SCID due to risk of disseminated infection.
references: Chinn IK, Orange JS. Immunodeficiency Disorders. Pediatr Rev. 2019;40(5):229-242. doi:10.1542/pir.2017-0308
Lee AY, Gray PE. Evaluating for immunodeficiency in children with recurrent infection. Aust Fam Physician. 2014;43(9):629-632
McCusker C, Upton J, Warrington R. Primary immunodeficiency. Allergy Asthma Clin Immunol. 2018;14(Suppl 2):61. Published 2018 Sep 12. doi:10.1186/s13223-018-0290-5
